text:
Computer Organization
3 Fundamental Components of Computer
• The CPU (ALU, Control Unit, Registers)
• The Memory Subsystem (Stored Data)
• The I/O subsystem (I/O devices)
2
I/O Device
Subsystem
Address Bus
Data Bus
Control Bus
CPU Memory
Subsystem
Each of these Components are connected 
through Buses.
• BUS - Physically a set of wires. The components of the Computer are 
connected to these buses.
• Address Bus
• Data Bus
• Control Bus
3
Address Bus
• Used to specify the address of the memory location to access.
• Each I/O devices has a unique address. (monitor, mouse, cd-rom)
• CPU reads data or instructions from other locations by specifying the 
address of its location.
• CPU always outputs to the address bus and never reads from it.
4
Data Bus
• Actual data is transferred via the data bus.
• When the cpu sends an address to memory, the memory will send 
data via the data bus in return to the cpu.
5
Control Bus
• Collection of individual control signals.
• Whether the cpu will read or write data.
• CPU is accessing memory or an I/O device
• Memory or I/O is ready to transfer data
6
I/O Bus or Local Bus
• In today’s computers the the I/O controller will have an extra bus 
called the I/O bus.
• The I/O bus will be used to access all other I/O devices connected to 
the system.
• Example: PCI bus
7
Instruction Cycles
• Procedure the CPU goes through to process an instruction.
• 1. Fetch - get instruction
• 2. Decode - interperate the instruction
• 3. Execute - run the instruction.
8
CPU organization
• CPU controls the Computer
subject: notes

text:
e CPU goes through to process an instruction.
• 1. Fetch - get instruction
• 2. Decode - interperate the instruction
• 3. Execute - run the instruction.
8
CPU organization
• CPU controls the Computer
• The CPU will fetch, decode and execute instructions.
• The CPU has three internal sections: register section, ALU and 
Control Unit
9
Register Section
• Includes collection of registers and a bus.
• Processor’s instruction set architecture are found in this section.
• Non accessible registers by the programmer. These are to be used for 
registers to latch the address being accessed and a temp storage 
register.
10
Arithmetic/Logic Unit (ALU)
• Performs most Arithmetic and logical operations.
• Retrieves and stores its information with the register section of the 
CPU.
11
Memory
Main memory consists of a number of 
storage locations, each of which is 
identified by a unique address
The ability of the CPU to identify each 
location is known as its addressability
Each location stores a word i.e. the 
number of bits that can be processed by 
the CPU in a single operation.  Word 
length may be typically 16, 24, 32 or as 
many as 64 bits.
A large word length improves system 
performance, though may be less efficient on 
occasions when the full word length is not 
used
12
Memory Subsystem
• 2 Types of Memory:
• ROM : Read Only Memory
• Program that is loaded into memory and cannot be changed also retains its data even 
without power.
• RAM : Random Access Memory
• Also called read/write memory. This type of memory can have a program loaded and
subject: notes

text:
is loaded into memory and cannot be changed also retains its data even 
without power.
• RAM : Random Access Memory
• Also called read/write memory. This type of memory can have a program loaded and 
then reloaded. It also loses its data with no power.
13
Different ROM Chips
•Masked ROM : 
• ROM that is programmed with data when fabricated. Data will not change once 
installed. Hardwired.
•Programmable ROM (PROM) :
• Capable of being programmed by the user with a ROM programmer. Not hardwired.
•Erasable PROM (EPROM) :
• Much like the PROM this EPROM can be programmed  and then erased by light.
•EEPROM : 
• Another form of EPROM but is reprogammable electrically.
14
Different RAM Chips
• Dynamic RAM (DRAM) : 
• Leaky capacitors. Caps are charged and slowly leak until they are refreshed to there 
original data locations. Ex. Computer RAM
• Static RAM (SRAM) :
• Much like a register. The contents stay valid and does not have to be refreshed. SRAM is 
faster than DRAM but cost more Ex. Cache
• Each RAM chip has 2^n * m.  n address inputs and m bidirectional data pins
15
The operation of cache memory
1. Cache fetches data from 
next to current addresses in 
main memory
2. CPU checks to see 
whether the next instruction 
it requires is in cache
3. If it is, then the 
instruction is fetched from 
the cache – a very fast 
position
4. If not, the CPU has to 
fetch next instruction from 
main memory -  a much 
slower process

Immediate Addressing
• Operand is part of instruction
• Operand = address field
• e.g. ADD 5
• Add 5 to contents of accumulator
• 5 is operand
• No memory reference to fetch data
• Fast
• Limited range
18

Direct Addressing
• Address field contains address of operand
• Effective address (EA) = address field (A)
• e.g.  ADD A
• Add contents of cell A to accumulator
• Look in memory at address A for operand
• Single memory reference to access data
• No additional calculations to work out effective address
• Limited address space

Indirect Addressing (1)
• Memory cell pointed to by address field contains the address of 
(pointer to) the operand
• EA = (A)
• Look in A, find address (A) and look there for operand
•e.g. ADD (A)
• Add contents of cell pointed to by contents of A to accumulator

Indirect Addressing (2)
• Large address space 
• 2n where n = word length
• May be nested, multilevel, cascaded
• e.g. EA = (((A)))

Base-Register Addressing
• A holds displacement
• R holds pointer to base address
• R may be explicit or implicit
• e.g. segment registers in 80x86
34
Indexed Addressing
• A = base
• R = displacement
• EA = A + (R)
• Good for accessing arrays
• EA = A + (R)
• R++
35
Stack Addressing
• Operand is (implicitly) on top of stack
• e.g. 
• ADD Pop top two items from stack    and add
36
Input/output Devices
• Input/output devices are required for users to communicate with the 
computer.
• In simple terms, input devices bring information INTO the computer 
and output devices bring information OUT of a computer system. 
These input/output devices are also known as peripherals.
37
Input Devices are:
• Keyboard
• Mouse
• Joystick
• Scanner
• Light Pen
• Touch Screen
38

Output devices are:
• Printers
• Plotters
• Monitor 
• LCD

text:
GAUSS–JORDAN ELIMINATION (SHORT + 2 EXAMPLES)

STEPS:

1) Convert equations → Augmented Matrix [A | B]

2) For each column:
   a) Make pivot = 1
      Ri → Ri / pivot
   b) Make all other entries in that column = 0
      Rj → Rj − (value)Ri

3) Continue until matrix becomes:
      [1 0 0 | x]
      [0 1 0 | y]
      [0 0 1 | z]

4) Read solution from last column.


------------------------------------------------
EXAMPLE 1 (2×2)

x + y = 3
2x + y = 4

[ 1  1 | 3 ]
[ 2  1 | 4 ]

R2 → R2 − 2R1
[ 1  1 | 3 ]
[ 0 -1 | -2 ]

R2 → R2 / (-1)
[ 1  1 | 3 ]
[ 0  1 | 2 ]

R1 → R1 − R2
[ 1  0 | 1 ]
[ 0  1 | 2 ]

Answer:  x=1 , y=2


------------------------------------------------
EXAMPLE 2 (3×3)

x + y + z = 6
2x − y + z = 3
x + 2y − z = 3

STEP 1: Augmented Matrix
[ 1  1  1 | 6 ]
[ 2 -1  1 | 3 ]
[ 1  2 -1 | 3 ]

STEP 2: Make zeros below first pivot
R2 → R2 − 2R1
R3 → R3 − R1

[ 1  1  1 | 6 ]
[ 0 -3 -1 | -9 ]
[ 0  1 -2 | -3 ]

STEP 3: Make second pivot = 1
R2 → R2 / (-3)

[ 1  1   1 | 6 ]
[ 0  1  1/3 | 3 ]
[ 0  1  -2 | -3 ]

STEP 4: Make zeros in column 2
R1 → R1 − R2
R3 → R3 − R2

[ 1  0  2/3 | 3 ]
[ 0  1  1/3 | 3 ]
[ 0  0 -7/3 | -6 ]

STEP 5: Make third pivot = 1
R3 → R3 / (-7/3)  → multiply by (-3/7)

[ 1  0  2/3 | 3 ]
[ 0  1  1/3 | 3 ]
[ 0  0   1  | 18/7 ]

STEP 6: Make zeros above third pivot
R1 → R1 − (2/3)R3
R2 → R2 − (1/3)R3

[ 1  0  0 | 9/7 ]
[ 0  1  0 | 15/7 ]
[ 0  0  1 | 18/7 ]

FINAL ANSWER:
x = 9/7
y = 15/7
z = 18/7

text:
Subject: Mathematics
Chapter: 1 - Algebra

METHOD: Solving a linear equation in one variable

Step 1: Collect all terms containing the variable on one side of the equation and constants on the other.
Step 2: Add or subtract the same quantity from both sides to isolate the variable term.
Step 3: Multiply or divide both sides by the coefficient of the variable to get the solution.
Step 4: Substitute the solution back into the original equation to verify.

Example:
Solve 2x + 3 = 7.
Step 1: 2x = 7 - 3 = 4
Step 2: (already isolated)
Step 3: x = 4/2 = 2
Step 4: 2(2) + 3 = 7. Correct.

--- Additional Topics ---

Topic: Quadratic equations
Method:
1. Move all terms to one side to get ax^2 + bx + c = 0.
2. Use factorization, completing the square, or the quadratic formula x = (-b ± sqrt(b^2 - 4ac)) / (2a).
3. Verify roots by substitution.

Topic: Factoring polynomials
Method:
1. Look for common factors.
2. Use grouping for four-term polynomials.
3. Apply formulas (a^2 - b^2 = (a-b)(a+b), perfect square trinomials).

Topic: Solving systems of linear equations
Method:
1. Use substitution or elimination to reduce to one equation in one variable.
2. Alternatively, use matrix methods (Gaussian elimination).

Topic: Basic differentiation (calculus)
Method:
1. Use power rule: d/dx x^n = n x^(n-1).
2. Apply product, quotient, and chain rules as needed.

Topic: Basic integration
Method:
1. Use power rule for integrals: ∫ x^n dx = x^(n+1)/(n+1) + C (n ≠ -1).
2. Use substitution and integration by parts when applicable.
subject: notes

Topic: Basic integration
Method:
1. Use power rule for integrals: ∫ x^n dx = x^(n+1)/(n+1) + C (n ≠ -1).
2. Use substitution and integration by parts when applicable.

Topic: Trigonometry identities
Method:
1. Memorize key identities: sin^2 x + cos^2 x = 1, angle addition formulas.
2. Use identities to simplify expressions and solve equations.

Topic: Geometry — area formulas
Method:
1. Triangle: A = 1/2 * base * height.
2. Circle: A = π r^2.
3. Rectangle: A = length * width.

Topic: Probability basics
Method:
1. P(A) = favorable outcomes / total outcomes.
2. For independent events, P(A and B) = P(A)P(B).

Topic: Sequences and series
Method:
1. Identify arithmetic (difference constant) vs geometric (ratio constant) sequences.
2. Arithmetic: nth term a_n = a_1 + (n-1)d, sum S_n = n/2 (2a_1 + (n-1)d).
3. Geometric: nth term a_n = a_1 r^(n-1), sum S_n = a_1(1-r^n)/(1-r). Infinite geometric converges if |r|<1.

Topic: Matrices
Method:
1. Operations: addition, scalar multiplication, matrix multiplication.
2. Determinant and inverse for square matrices; use inverse or row reduction to solve linear systems.
3. Eigenvalues and eigenvectors (basic idea and computation for small matrices).

Topic: Complex numbers
Method:
1. Write z = a + bi; conjugate z̄ = a - bi; modulus |z| = sqrt(a^2 + b^2).
2. Use polar form r(cos θ + i sin θ) and De Moivre's theorem for powers and roots.
3. Perform arithmetic and solve equations with complex roots.
subject: notes

2. Use polar form r(cos θ + i sin θ) and De Moivre's theorem for powers and roots.
3. Perform arithmetic and solve equations with complex roots.

Topic: Logarithms and exponents
Method:
1. Laws: a^m a^n = a^(m+n); log(ab)=log a + log b, etc.
2. Solve exponential and logarithmic equations by rewriting forms.
3. Use change of base: log_b a = log_k a / log_k b.

Topic: Limits (calculus)
Method:
1. Evaluate limits by substitution when possible; factor or rationalize for indeterminate forms.
2. Use L'Hôpital's rule for 0/0 or ∞/∞ cases.
3. Understand limits as foundation for continuity and derivatives.

Topic: Statistics — descriptive
Method:
1. Mean, median, and mode definitions and calculation.
2. Variance and standard deviation formulas and interpretation.
3. Understand skewness and basic distribution shapes.

Topic: Combinatorics — counting
Method:
1. Permutations: n! / (n-k)! for ordered selections.
2. Combinations: n choose k = n! / (k!(n-k)!) for unordered selections.
3. Use inclusion-exclusion for overlapping counts.

Topic: Number theory basics
Method:
1. Compute GCD using Euclidean algorithm; LCM via product/GCD relation.
2. Prime factorization and its uses.
3. Work with modular arithmetic and congruences.

Topic: Vectors
Method:
1. Represent vectors, add, subtract, and scale.
2. Dot product (gives projection), cross product (3D, gives area and perpendicular vector).
3. Apply vectors to geometry and physics problems.
subject: notes

text:
Method:
1. Represent vectors, add, subtract, and scale.
2. Dot product (gives projection), cross product (3D, gives area and perpendicular vector).
3. Apply vectors to geometry and physics problems.

Topic: Proof by induction
Method:
1. Prove base case (usually n=1 or 0).
2. Assume statement for n (inductive hypothesis).
3. Prove for n+1 using the hypothesis.

Topic: Inequalities
Method:
1. Solve by isolating terms; reverse inequality when multiplying/dividing by negative numbers.
2. Use common inequalities: AM-GM, Cauchy-Schwarz for bounds.
3. Solve linear and quadratic inequalities using sign analysis or intervals.

Topic: Coordinate geometry
Method:
1. Line: slope-intercept y = mx + b; slope m = (y2-y1)/(x2-x1).
2. Distance formula: sqrt((x2-x1)^2 + (y2-y1)^2); midpoint formula.
3. Equations of circles and conic sections (parabola, ellipse, hyperbola basics).

Topic: Transformations (geometry)
Method:
1. Definitions: translation, rotation, reflection, dilation.
2. Represent linear transformations with matrices.
3. Compose transformations and understand effects on shapes.

Topic: Set theory — basics
Method:
1. Definitions: union, intersection, complement, subset.
2. Use Venn diagrams to visualize relationships.
3. Apply set identities and De Morgan's laws.

Topic: Binomial theorem
Method:
1. Expand (a + b)^n = sum_{k=0}^n C(n,k) a^{n-k} b^k, where C(n,k) = n choose k.
2. Use Pascal's triangle to find coefficients.
3. Apply to problems involving coefficients and expansions.

address is given by DE register pair i.e. 5748 H (10 H) to the accumulator.
